title: Core Certificate Engine
description: Core cryptographic logic for ShushTLS. Generate a self-signed root CA, issue wildcard leaf certificates signed by that CA, persist all material to disk, and reload on startup. No HTTP concerns — this is the pure certificate engine layer.
notes: Scope expanded post-implementation: adding multi-cert support (shushtls-3zu4). The engine now needs to support issuing and persisting multiple wildcard and FQDN certs, not just one of each.
close_reason: All tasks complete including multi-cert refactor. Certificate engine supports CA generation, arbitrary cert issuance (wildcard + FQDN), disk persistence, reload, and idempotency.

title: Define certificate parameters and defaults
description: Define all cryptographic parameters: key algorithms (e.g. ECDSA P-256 or RSA 2048+), root CA validity (20-30 years), leaf validity (10+ years), wildcard SAN pattern (*.home.arpa), key usages, extended key usages, serial number generation strategy. These are constants/config the rest of the engine depends on.
close_reason: Defined in params.go: ECDSA P-256, 25yr root CA, 10yr leaf, home.arpa default, key usages

title: Implement root CA generation
description: Implement self-signed root CA generation. Generate a private key and self-signed X.509 certificate with CA:TRUE, appropriate key usages (certSign, crlSign), and the configured long validity period. Return the key pair and certificate as in-memory objects (no disk I/O in this layer).
close_reason: Implemented in ca.go: self-signed root CA with GenerateCA()

title: Implement wildcard leaf certificate issuance
description: Implement leaf certificate generation signed by the root CA. Generate a new key pair, create a certificate with the wildcard SAN (e.g. *.home.arpa), appropriate key usages (digitalSignature, keyEncipherment), extended key usage (serverAuth), and the configured validity. The issuing CA key and cert are inputs.
notes: Scope expanded: the original single-wildcard implementation is being replaced by a multi-cert model (shushtls-3zu4). The underlying issueLeafForHosts function remains, but the engine/store wrappers are being generalized.
close_reason: Implemented in leaf.go: IssueWildcard() and IssueServiceCert()

title: Persist keys and certificates to disk
description: Implement PEM encoding and disk persistence for all cryptographic material: root CA key, root CA cert, leaf key, leaf cert. Define a clear on-disk layout. Use safe file writing (write-then-rename or restrictive permissions on keys). Private keys should be written with 0600 permissions.
close_reason: Implemented in store.go: PEM encoding, atomic writes, 0600 key perms

title: Load existing material on startup
description: Implement loading of existing root CA and leaf certificates from disk on startup. Parse PEM files, validate that loaded material is well-formed (valid x509, key matches cert). Return structured state indicating what exists and what is missing.
close_reason: Implemented in store.go Load* methods + engine.load() with key/cert validation

title: Enforce idempotent behavior
description: Ensure the certificate engine never accidentally regenerates existing material. If a root CA already exists on disk, generation must be a no-op or an explicit error. Same for leaf certificates. Idempotency prevents catastrophic trust breakage if the service restarts or the user double-clicks initialize.
close_reason: Engine.Initialize() and GenerateWildcard() are idempotent, tested in engine_test.go

title: Application Bootstrapping Logic
description: Bootstrap logic that detects first-run vs initialized state and enforces the correct serving mode. HTTP-only before root CA exists, HTTPS-only after. Prevents TLS deadlocks and invalid mixed states. Defines restart requirements.
close_reason: All bootstrap tasks complete: state detection, HTTP-only/HTTPS-only mode enforcement, mixed state prevention, restart semantics. 4 server tests passing.

title: Detect first-run vs initialized state
description: Implement state detection logic: check disk for root CA material. Define a clear enum/type for system state (Uninitialized, Initialized, Ready). Uninitialized = no root CA. Initialized = root CA exists but no leaf cert for ShushTLS itself. Ready = all material present for HTTPS serving.
close_reason: State detection wired into server via certengine.State()

title: Enforce HTTP-only mode before initialization
description: When state is Uninitialized, the service must only listen on HTTP. The HTTP server serves the setup UI and API endpoints for initialization. No TLS listener should be created. This is the entry point for first-time users.
close_reason: Server.runHTTP serves HTTP-only when Uninitialized or Initialized

title: Enforce HTTPS-only mode after initialization
description: When state is Ready (root CA + ShushTLS leaf cert exist), the service must only listen on HTTPS using the ShushTLS leaf certificate. The leaf cert must be signed by the root CA, not self-signed. HTTP should either redirect to HTTPS or not be served at all.
close_reason: Server.runHTTPS serves HTTPS-only when Ready, using CA-signed service cert

title: Prevent invalid mixed states
description: Add defensive checks: never serve HTTPS without valid certs, never serve HTTP after initialization is complete. Handle edge cases like partial initialization (root CA exists but leaf does not) gracefully — either complete the setup or present clear guidance.

title: Refactor certificate engine to support multiple certs
description: The current engine hardcodes one wildcard and one service cert. Refactor to support issuing and persisting an arbitrary number of certificates (both wildcards and FQDNs), keyed by their primary SAN. Replace fixed-slot store methods with generic SaveCert/LoadCert/ListCerts/CertPaths. Replace Engine.GenerateWildcard with a general IssueCert(dnsNames). The service cert becomes just another entry in the map that ShushTLS looks up by hostname. On-disk layout changes from flat files to one subdirectory per cert under certs/, with * sanitized to _wildcard_ in directory names.

Once initialized, these are baked into the CA cert permanently.

title: Optional basic auth for API
description:
Add optional HTTP Basic Auth that can be enabled after initialization via POST /api/auth. When enabled, write/sensitive endpoints require valid credentials. Read-only certificate endpoints remain unprotected so install scripts can fetch certs without credentials. Specifically:

Protected (when auth enabled):
- POST /api/initialize
- POST /api/certificates
- POST /api/auth
- GET /api/status
- GET /api/certificates/{san}?type=key (private key downloads)

Unprotected (always open):
- GET /api/ca/root.pem (root CA download)
- GET /api/certificates (cert listing)
- GET /api/certificates/{san} (cert download, not keys)

Credentials stored in state/auth.json with bcrypt-hashed passwords. Can be disabled via POST /api/auth with {"enabled": false} (while authenticated) or by deleting state/auth.json and restarting. Auth is off by default and requires initialization before it can be enabled.

title: Root CA install helper scripts via API
description:
Add API endpoints that return ready-to-run shell commands or scripts for installing the ShushTLS root CA into the system trust store. The user curls the endpoint and pipes it to their shell (or copies the command). Platform-specific endpoints or a single endpoint with platform detection:

- GET /api/ca/install/macos — outputs a shell script using 'security add-trusted-cert'
- GET /api/ca/install/linux — outputs a shell script for update-ca-certificates (Debian/Ubuntu) and update-ca-trust (RHEL/Fedora)
- GET /api/ca/install/windows — outputs a PowerShell script using Import-Certificate

Each script should:
1. Download the root CA PEM from the ShushTLS instance (self-referencing URL)
2. Install it into the appropriate system trust store
3. Print clear success/failure output

These endpoints should remain unprotected by auth (like other cert read endpoints) so they can be curled freely. Consider also a GET /api/ca/install that returns a summary of available platforms with example commands.

Could be a POST /api/service-cert endpoint that regenerates with new hostnames, or a way to designate an existing issued cert as the service cert. Requires a restart to take effect.

title: Generate fresh certs on download instead of storing them
description:
Browser-accepted TLS cert validity keeps shrinking per CA/Browser Forum ballot SC-081:

- Until 2026-03-15: max 398 days
- From  2026-03-15: max 200 days
- From  2027-03-15: max 100 days
- From  2029-03-15: max  47 days

At 47 days, manual cert management is untenable. ShushTLS should generate fresh leaf certs on each download rather than storing them permanently. The CA and the SAN configuration (DNS names) are the only things that need to persist. When a user or device fetches a cert, ShushTLS issues a new one on the fly, signed by the root CA, with the appropriate maximum validity for the current date. This eliminates cert expiry management entirely — just re-download when needed.

The code should be aware of these dates and automatically select the correct maximum validity based on the current date.

Needs careful thought about the service cert (ShushTLS's own HTTPS cert) which also needs periodic auto-rotation.
close_reason: Leaf certs generated on download with SC081MaxLeafValidity(); only CA and SAN config persisted; service cert uses SC-081 and auto-rotates when over max; DesignateServiceCert can promote on-demand SAN to service.

title: Support ACME protocol for certificate renewal
description:
ShushTLS should act as an ACME server and let clients renew their certs via the ACME protocol (e.g. HTTP-01 or DNS-01 challenges). Clients could use certbot, acme.sh, or other ACME clients to obtain and automatically renew certificates from ShushTLS.

ShushTLS should not give a flying fuck about the challenges—just respond positively no matter what.

title: Improve agent accessibility
description:
Agents (LLM-based and otherwise) integrating with ShushTLS currently have to discover behavior empirically — fetching pages, scraping docs, issuing throwaway certs to inspect them. A real-world session integrating ShushTLS with a CockroachDB cluster on Kubernetes (via the cockroach-operator) surfaced a clear pattern: every fumble was a missing piece of machine-readable contract or undocumented quirk. This epic groups the concrete issues that came out of that session.

Goal: an agent (or human) handed only the base URL should be able to: (a) discover the full API in one fetch, (b) understand the cert profile without issuing a test cert, (c) integrate with k8s/cert-manager without hitting undocumented traps, and (d) issue certs that fit non-web-server use cases (etcd, CockroachDB, Kubernetes-internal services where CN matters).

Children break this down into focused, independently shippable changes.

title: Serve OpenAPI 3.x spec at /api/openapi.json
description:
## Why
The fastest way for an agent to learn the API is to ingest a machine-readable schema. Right now every endpoint, parameter, response shape, auth requirement, and error format has to be inferred from scraping /docs (which is HTML, lossy, and incomplete). One JSON fetch should replace that.

## What
Publish an OpenAPI 3.x document describing every endpoint registered in internal/api/api.go and internal/acme/server.go.

Endpoints to cover (from internal/api/api.go:81-96):
- GET /api/status (auth)
- POST /api/initialize (auth)
- POST /api/certificates (auth) — issue
- POST /api/service-cert (auth)
- POST /api/auth (auth)
- GET/PUT /api/leaf-subject (auth)
- GET /api/ca/root.pem (no auth)
- GET /api/certificates (no auth) — list
- GET /api/certificates/{primary_san} (auth for bundle, no auth for metadata?) — download
- GET /api/ca/install (no auth)
- GET /api/ca/install/{macos,linux,windows} (no auth)

For each: HTTP method, path, query/path/body params with types, auth requirement, response schema (success + error), examples.

ACME endpoints (internal/acme/server.go:90-98) — out of scope for OpenAPI since ACME has its own RFC8555 contract, but link to /acme/directory from the OpenAPI 'externalDocs'.

## Acceptance
- GET /api/openapi.json returns a valid OpenAPI 3.x document (validates with redocly or openapi-validator)
- All current /api endpoints documented with realistic example bodies
- Linked from /docs UI
- Spec is unit-tested for validity in internal/api/api_test.go
acceptance: GET /api/openapi.json returns a spec that passes openapi-validator with zero errors and covers every endpoint registered in api.go

title: Document leaf certificate profile (EKU, KU, key algorithm)
description:
## Why
Integrators currently have to issue a throwaway cert and run `openssl x509 -text` to learn:
- KeyUsage = digitalSignature (only)
- ExtKeyUsage = serverAuth + clientAuth (both, always)
- Key algorithm = ECDSA P-256
- Signature algorithm = ecdsa-with-SHA256
- Default validity = (whatever it is — also undocumented)

Software that's picky about cert profile (CockroachDB, etcd, MongoDB, mTLS-strict middleware) needs to know this before adopting ShushTLS.

## What
Add a 'Certificate profile' section to /docs (template: internal/ui/templates/docs.html) that states:
- Key type and curve (from internal/certengine/leaf.go:66 — ecdsa P-256)
- KeyUsage and ExtKeyUsage values (from internal/certengine/params.go:145,150 — LeafKeyUsages, LeafExtKeyUsages)
- Signature algorithm
- Default validity period and where it's configured
- Subject construction rules (CN = primary SAN; O/OU/C/L/ST from /api/leaf-subject defaults)
- Wildcard SAN expansion behavior (from internal/certengine/leaf.go:64 — `expanded := expandWildcardSANs(dnsNames)` — *.example.com also includes example.com)

## Acceptance
- /docs page renders a 'Certificate profile' section that lists every property above with the values currently produced by the engine
- Section explicitly notes that both serverAuth and clientAuth EKU are present on every cert (so the cert works as both server and client without configuration)

title: Allow per-certificate subject override (custom CN)
description:
## Why
Currently CN is hardcoded to dnsNames[0] (internal/certengine/leaf.go:97 — `CommonName: dnsNames[0]`) and O/OU/C/L/ST come from the global /api/leaf-subject defaults. This breaks for any system that uses CN as identity:

- **CockroachDB** node certs must have CN=`node` to be accepted as a peer node identity. Workaround required: --cert-principal-map=<san>:node flag on every node, plus a node cert whose primary SAN doubles as the principal map source. Fragile.
- **CockroachDB** root SQL user requires CN=`root`. Workaround: issue a cert with primary SAN 'root' — abuses the SAN list to drive CN.
- **etcd** peer/client certs likewise have CN-based identity expectations.
- **Kubernetes** kubelet client certs map CN→user.

## What
On POST /api/certificates, accept optional fields:
```
{
  "dns_names": [...],
  "common_name": "node",            // overrides CN; defaults to dns_names[0]
  "subject": {                         // overrides leaf-subject defaults for this cert only
    "organization": "...",
    "organizational_unit": "...",
    "country": "...",
    "locality": "...",
    "province": "..."
  }
}
```

CN can be any string (no requirement that it appears in dns_names) — this is what enables CN=node/root for non-DNS principals.

Implementation: internal/api/api.go handleIssueCert reads new fields and passes them to internal/certengine/leaf.go IssueLeaf alongside dnsNames. Add CN/subject overrides to the LeafParams struct in internal/certengine/params.go.

## Acceptance
- POST /api/certificates with {"dns_names":["cockroachdb.example"],"common_name":"node"} produces a cert with Subject CN=node and SAN=cockroachdb.example
- Existing call shape (no common_name field) still produces CN=dns_names[0] — backwards compatible
- Subject override is per-cert and does not mutate the global leaf-subject defaults
- OpenAPI spec updated (depends on shushtls-5ism)

title: Accept CSR submission for callers with pre-existing private keys
description:
## Why
The current POST /api/certificates flow generates the private key server-side and returns it in the bundle. That's the right default for the home-LAN use case where the CA operator and the device owner are the same person.

But two real scenarios on a home network can't use that flow:
1. **Hardware-backed keys** — a TPM-, Secure-Enclave-, or YubiKey-stored key can never leave the device. The device needs to generate the key locally and only ask ShushTLS to sign it.
2. **Workloads that generate their own keys** — Kubernetes pods using cert-manager with a private CSR, etcd nodes provisioning themselves, mTLS daemons that mint a fresh keypair on boot. They already have the key; they need a cert for it.

ShushTLS already has CSR plumbing internally — the ACME finalize path (internal/acme/server.go) uses internal/certengine/csr.go to sign caller-supplied CSRs. This issue exposes the same capability on the REST API.

## What
Add a content-type branch to POST /api/certificates:
- `Content-Type: application/json` → existing flow (key generated server-side). No change for existing callers.
- `Content-Type: application/pkcs10` or PEM body → sign the submitted CSR, return cert only (no private key in response).

Validation: enforce the existing leaf cert profile (LeafKeyUsages, LeafExtKeyUsages from internal/certengine/params.go) regardless of what the CSR requests. Reject CSRs with CA:true or unexpected extensions.

## Acceptance
- POST /api/certificates with a PEM-encoded CSR body returns the signed cert
- Returned cert's SANs match the CSR's SANs; profile (KU/EKU) comes from the engine constants, not the CSR
- Existing JSON flow unchanged
- /docs documents both flows side-by-side, with the JSON flow as the recommended default

title: Bundle ergonomics: include CA, use generic filenames, optional k8s Secret output
description:
## Why
The current bundle from GET /api/certificates/{primary_san}?type=tar contains exactly two files named after the primary SAN:
- `{primary_san}.cert.pem`
- `{primary_san}.key.pem`

Two consequences for integrators:
1. Every script needs to know the primary SAN to find the files. Generic automation ("unpack and apply") needs an extra parameter or globbing.
2. The bundle does NOT include the root CA — every caller has to make a separate request to GET /api/ca/root.pem and stitch it together. Two HTTPS round-trips and two failure modes instead of one.

In the CockroachDB-on-k8s integration this manifested as a CronJob script that has to know the primary SAN per cert *and* fetch ca.crt separately *and* assemble three files into a Kubernetes Secret. With better bundle output it could be a four-line script.

## What
1. **Include the CA in the bundle.** Add `ca.pem` (root CA in PEM) to every tar/zip. Optional: also add `chain.pem` (cert+CA concatenated) for servers that prefer a chain file.

2. **Generic filenames.** Add a query parameter or new endpoint that uses canonical names: `cert.pem`, `key.pem`, `ca.pem` (or `chain.pem`). Keep the existing primary-SAN-named layout for backwards compat. Suggested API: `?names=generic` or a new bundle format.

3. **Optional Kubernetes Secret output.** `?format=k8s-tls` returns a ready-to-apply Secret YAML:
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
type: kubernetes.io/tls
metadata:
  name: {primary_san}
data:
  tls.crt: <base64>
  tls.key: <base64>
  ca.crt: <base64>
```
Lets a caller `curl ... | kubectl apply -f -` — no scripting needed.

Implementation: internal/api/api.go handleGetCert (around api.go:385) currently constructs the tar. Add the additional file(s) and the format switch there.

## Acceptance
- Default bundle (existing format) still works for backwards compat
- New default OR new format includes ca.pem alongside cert.pem and key.pem
- ?format=k8s-tls returns a valid Kubernetes Secret YAML that can be piped to `kubectl apply -f -`
- Documented in /docs

title: Document the cert-manager self-check trap (and consider a webhook solver)
description:
## Why
ShushTLS's ACME server intentionally accepts any challenge response — internal/acme/server.go marks authorizations valid without contacting the requesting client. This works for acme.sh and certbot (which post a challenge response and trust the server's verdict) but **fails for cert-manager** because cert-manager performs a *local* self-check before telling the ACME server to validate:

1. cert-manager creates a solver pod + Service + Ingress for the challenge
2. cert-manager itself issues GET http://{dnsname}/.well-known/acme-challenge/{token} from inside the cluster
3. Only if that succeeds does cert-manager POST to the ACME server's challenge URL

For internal DNS names (`*.svc.cluster.local`, `root`, etc.) the self-check fails because the names don't resolve and the cluster has no ingress controller covering them. ShushTLS would happily mark valid — but cert-manager never asks. The challenge stays Pending forever. A real integration session burned ~45 minutes here before falling back to the direct API.

## What — Documentation
Add a 'Using with cert-manager' page or section under /acme that:
- States plainly: cert-manager's HTTP-01 self-check must succeed before cert-manager talks to the ACME server. ShushTLS's permissive validation does not help.
- Lists the realistic workarounds in order of effort:
  a) Use cert-manager's DNS-01 with a webhook solver that is similarly permissive
  b) Use a CronJob hitting POST /api/certificates directly (sample manifest)
  c) Install an ingress controller and a CoreDNS wildcard rewrite so the self-check resolves to the solver
- Includes a working CronJob YAML example for option (b) — the working manifest from the CockroachDB session at ~/shushtls-cert-refresh.yaml can serve as a starting point

## What — Optional code path
Ship a tiny cert-manager DNS-01 webhook (separate repo: cert-manager-webhook-shushtls) that always presents/checks DNS records as 'present'. cert-manager skips its own DNS propagation check when using a webhook solver that returns Present without delay. This unlocks the ACME path end-to-end inside Kubernetes.

## Acceptance
- /acme page (template internal/ui/templates/acme.html) gains a 'Using with cert-manager' section covering the trap and at least one working workaround with a copy-pasteable manifest
- Webhook solver tracked as a separate follow-up issue if scope grows

title: Document ACME directory URL on /docs
description:
## Why
The ACME directory URL is the entrypoint for every ACME client. It's served at `/acme/directory` (internal/acme/server.go:90 — `mux.HandleFunc("GET "+dir, s.handleDirectory)`) but the path is not stated on /docs.

The /acme page mentions the ACME server exists; /docs does not link to it; an agent has to either find /acme by following nav, or brute-force-probe common paths (`/acme/directory`, `/api/acme/directory`, `/acme/v1/directory`) to discover the right one. In testing it took four guesses.

## What
Add to /docs (template: internal/ui/templates/docs.html) under an 'ACME endpoint' subsection:
- Full directory URL (e.g. `https://your-host/acme/directory`)
- Cross-link to /acme page for setup details
- One-line note about challenge behavior (with link to the cert-manager trap doc — shushtls-4906)

## Acceptance
- /docs renders an 'ACME endpoint' section with the directory URL and a link to /acme
- Mention of the directory URL also appears in the OpenAPI externalDocs (depends on shushtls-5ism)

title: Document which ACME directory fields are intentionally omitted
description:
## Why
The directory endpoint at internal/acme/server.go:107-111 returns only:
```
{
  "newAccount": "...",
  "newNonce": "...",
  "newOrder": "..."
}
```

RFC8555 §7.1.1 lists additional fields most public CAs publish: `revokeCert`, `keyChange`, `newAuthz` (optional). For a home CA the omissions are mostly fine — revocation in a controlled LAN is 'just reissue', account key rotation is low-value when the threat model is friendly.

But callers who check the directory (stricter ACME clients, anyone debugging an integration) currently have no way to tell whether the omissions are bugs or by design. This caused real confusion during a cert-manager integration session.

## What
Update /acme (template: internal/ui/templates/acme.html) with a short 'What's supported' table listing each RFC8555 directory field and its status: implemented, intentionally omitted (with rationale), or considered. Recommended copy for the omissions:

- **revokeCert**: not implemented. On a home network, revocation is achieved by reissuing the cert under the same SANs (the old cert is removed from /api/certificates list and supersedes via expiry/replacement). If you need revocation as a primary mechanism, you probably need a CA bigger than ShushTLS.
- **keyChange**: not implemented. Account key rotation is low-value when account-key compromise on a trusted LAN already implies broader trust compromise.
- **newAuthz**: not implemented. Pre-authorization is unnecessary because challenges are auto-validated.

If a stricter client refuses to start without these fields, file an issue.

## Acceptance
- /acme page renders a 'What's supported' table covering at minimum: newAccount, newNonce, newOrder, newAuthz, revokeCert, keyChange
- Each omission has a one-sentence rationale aligned with the home-LAN trust model
- No code change to the ACME server; this is a docs-only issue

title: Enrich GET /api/certificates list response with cert metadata
description:
## Why
GET /api/certificates currently returns only:
```
[{
  "primary_san": "...",
  "dns_names": [...],
  "not_before": "...",
  "not_after": "...",
  "is_service": false
}]
```

To inspect anything else — Subject CN/O/OU, key algorithm, EKU, serial, SHA-256 fingerprint, signature algorithm — an agent has to download every bundle and run openssl. For inventory/audit use cases this is O(N) round-trips and bundle parsing instead of one JSON fetch.

## What
Extend the list response with cert metadata that's cheap to surface from the existing cert store (internal/certengine/store.go and the parsed cert in memory):
```
{
  "primary_san": "...",
  "common_name": "...",
  "dns_names": [...],
  "not_before": "...",
  "not_after": "...",
  "serial": "hex...",
  "sha256_fingerprint": "hex...",
  "key_algorithm": "ECDSA-P256",
  "signature_algorithm": "ECDSAWithSHA256",
  "key_usage": ["DigitalSignature"],
  "extended_key_usage": ["ServerAuth", "ClientAuth"],
  "is_service": false
}
```

These fields all come from x509.Certificate fields and are already parsed when the cert is loaded — no extra crypto work.

Optional: query param `?fields=basic` to keep the old shape, default to enriched.

## Acceptance
- GET /api/certificates returns the new fields for every cert
- Existing fields preserved (no breaking change for callers that look at primary_san / dns_names / not_after)
- OpenAPI spec reflects the richer shape (depends on shushtls-5ism)

title: Document the is_service field on certificate responses
description:
## Why
GET /api/certificates and POST /api/certificates both return an `is_service` boolean on each certificate object. It is not explained anywhere in /docs or /acme. Reading the source, the flag distinguishes the cert serving the ShushTLS API itself from the user-issued leaf certs (POST /api/service-cert at internal/api/api.go:84 sets/uses this).

An agent or admin looking at the list sees a mysterious boolean and has no way to interpret it without reading Go source.

## What
- Add a one-sentence definition of is_service to /docs (template: internal/ui/templates/docs.html), in the response schema section for /api/certificates.
- Include the same in the OpenAPI spec field description (depends on shushtls-5ism).

Suggested copy: `is_service: true if this is the certificate that ShushTLS itself serves on its API endpoint (managed via POST /api/service-cert); false for user-issued leaf certificates.`

## Acceptance
- /docs explains is_service in plain language
- OpenAPI description matches

title: Document root CA usage for callers that can't install via OS trust store
description:
## Why
ShushTLS already ships one-line installers (`/api/ca/install/{macos,linux,windows}`) that wire the root CA into the OS trust store — and that is the right primary UX. But some callers can't or shouldn't lean on the OS trust store:

- **cert-manager** ACME ClusterIssuer needs the root CA inline as `spec.acme.caBundle` (base64). In a real integration this took a probe of `shushtls.radical.org` with openssl to discover the API endpoint is self-signed, then base64-encoding the root PEM.
- **Container images** built from minimal bases (distroless, scratch) have no trust store to install into — they need a CA file mounted or baked in.
- **Bootstrap scripts** before the OS installer has run (e.g. provisioning a fresh host) need a way to pin the CA out-of-band.

Today there's no docs section answering 'how do I trust ShushTLS from a place where I can't run the OS installer?' and no fingerprint published for out-of-band verification.

## What
Add to /docs (template: internal/ui/templates/docs.html) a short 'Trusting the CA from constrained environments' section covering:
- The canonical fetch: `curl --insecure https://host/api/ca/root.pem -o ca.pem` (the --insecure is the bootstrap; subsequent calls use --cacert ca.pem)
- The root CA SHA-256 fingerprint — also surface it in the web UI on the install/trust page so a human can compare out-of-band
- One worked example each for: cert-manager (caBundle base64), Docker (COPY + SSL_CERT_FILE), Kubernetes ConfigMap mount

Explicitly NOT in scope: serving the API behind a public CA. ShushTLS is the CA — eating its own dogfood is the design.

## Acceptance
- /docs has a 'Trusting the CA from constrained environments' section with the bootstrap fetch, fingerprint, and three worked examples
- Root CA SHA-256 fingerprint visible in the web UI alongside the install button

title: Migrate REST API to Huma
description:
## Why

The HTTP API in `internal/api` is hand-written `net/http` handlers with ad-hoc JSON types and no machine-readable contract. Migrating to [Huma v2](https://huma.rocks/) while the surface is small (~15 routes) gives OpenAPI 3.x generation, JSON Schema, and guard rails before the API grows.

## Scope

- `internal/api` only (not ACME)
- `humago` on stdlib `ServeMux`; keep `certengine` / `auth` integration
- Child issues break down scaffold → JSON → binary → OpenAPI/docs → cleanup

## Epic outcome

All `/api/*` REST endpoints on Huma with parity tests passing; `GET /api/openapi.json` valid (supersedes hand-written approach in shushtls-5ism).

## Out of scope

- ACME in OpenAPI (link via externalDocs)
- New API features (CSR, enriched list, etc.) until migration is done
design:
Children (execution order):
1. shushtls-2qa0 — Record migration decisions (chore)
2. shushtls-3clz — Huma scaffold (task) ← blocked by 2qa0
3. shushtls-4bv5 + shushtls-21bw — JSON and binary/script migrations (parallel) ← blocked by 3clz
4. shushtls-3o84 — OpenAPI + docs (blocks shushtls-5ism) ← blocked by 4bv5 + 21bw
5. shushtls-1cef — Legacy cleanup + CI ← blocked by 3o84
acceptance:
- All child issues closed
- REST API fully on Huma with behavioral parity vs pre-migration
- shushtls-5ism satisfied via generated spec (close or fold into children)
notes: Restructured from single feature into epic with sub-issues.

- Error format documented (RFC 9457 problem+json vs legacy {"error"})

description.new:
Agents (LLM-based and otherwise) integrating with ShushTLS currently have to discover behavior empirically — fetching pages, scraping docs, issuing throwaway certs to inspect them. A real-world session integrating ShushTLS with a CockroachDB cluster on Kubernetes (via the cockroach-operator) surfaced a clear pattern: every fumble was a missing piece of machine-readable contract or undocumented quirk. This epic groups the concrete issues that came out of that session.

Goal: an agent (or human) handed only the base URL should be able to: (a) discover the full API in one fetch, (b) understand the cert profile without issuing a test cert, (c) integrate with k8s/cert-manager without hitting undocumented traps, and (d) issue certs that fit non-web-server use cases (etcd, CockroachDB, Kubernetes-internal services where CN matters).

Children break this down into focused, independently shippable changes.

title.new: Improve agent accessibility

acceptance.new: GET /api/openapi.json returns a spec that passes openapi-validator with zero errors and covers every endpoint registered in api.go

description.new:
## Why
The fastest way for an agent to learn the API is to ingest a machine-readable schema. Right now every endpoint, parameter, response shape, auth requirement, and error format has to be inferred from scraping /docs (which is HTML, lossy, and incomplete). One JSON fetch should replace that.

## What
Publish an OpenAPI 3.x document describing every endpoint registered in internal/api/api.go and internal/acme/server.go.

Endpoints to cover (from internal/api/api.go:81-96):
- GET /api/status (auth)
- POST /api/initialize (auth)
- POST /api/certificates (auth) — issue
- POST /api/service-cert (auth)
- POST /api/auth (auth)
- GET/PUT /api/leaf-subject (auth)
- GET /api/ca/root.pem (no auth)
- GET /api/certificates (no auth) — list
- GET /api/certificates/{primary_san} (auth for bundle, no auth for metadata?) — download
- GET /api/ca/install (no auth)
- GET /api/ca/install/{macos,linux,windows} (no auth)

For each: HTTP method, path, query/path/body params with types, auth requirement, response schema (success + error), examples.

ACME endpoints (internal/acme/server.go:90-98) — out of scope for OpenAPI since ACME has its own RFC8555 contract, but link to /acme/directory from the OpenAPI 'externalDocs'.

## Acceptance
- GET /api/openapi.json returns a valid OpenAPI 3.x document (validates with redocly or openapi-validator)
- All current /api endpoints documented with realistic example bodies
- Linked from /docs UI
- Spec is unit-tested for validity in internal/api/api_test.go

Document leaf certificate profile (EKU, KU, key algorithm)

description.new:
## Why
Integrators currently have to issue a throwaway cert and run `openssl x509 -text` to learn:
- KeyUsage = digitalSignature (only)
- ExtKeyUsage = serverAuth + clientAuth (both, always)
- Key algorithm = ECDSA P-256
- Signature algorithm = ecdsa-with-SHA256
- Default validity = (whatever it is — also undocumented)

Software that's picky about cert profile (CockroachDB, etcd, MongoDB, mTLS-strict middleware) needs to know this before adopting ShushTLS.

## What
Add a 'Certificate profile' section to /docs (template: internal/ui/templates/docs.html) that states:
- Key type and curve (from internal/certengine/leaf.go:66 — ecdsa P-256)
- KeyUsage and ExtKeyUsage values (from internal/certengine/params.go:145,150 — LeafKeyUsages, LeafExtKeyUsages)
- Signature algorithm
- Default validity period and where it's configured
- Subject construction rules (CN = primary SAN; O/OU/C/L/ST from /api/leaf-subject defaults)
- Wildcard SAN expansion behavior (from internal/certengine/leaf.go:64 — `expanded := expandWildcardSANs(dnsNames)` — *.example.com also includes example.com)

## Acceptance
- /docs page renders a 'Certificate profile' section that lists every property above with the values currently produced by the engine
- Section explicitly notes that both serverAuth and clientAuth EKU are present on every cert (so the cert works as both server and client without configuration)

title: Document leaf certificate profile (EKU, KU, key algorithm)

description.new:
## Why
Currently CN is hardcoded to dnsNames[0] (internal/certengine/leaf.go:97 — `CommonName: dnsNames[0]`) and O/OU/C/L/ST come from the global /api/leaf-subject defaults. This breaks for any system that uses CN as identity:

- **CockroachDB** node certs must have CN=`node` to be accepted as a peer node identity. Workaround required: --cert-principal-map=<san>:node flag on every node, plus a node cert whose primary SAN doubles as the principal map source. Fragile.
- **CockroachDB** root SQL user requires CN=`root`. Workaround: issue a cert with primary SAN 'root' — abuses the SAN list to drive CN.
- **etcd** peer/client certs likewise have CN-based identity expectations.
- **Kubernetes** kubelet client certs map CN→user.

## What
On POST /api/certificates, accept optional fields:
```
{
  "dns_names": [...],
  "common_name": "node",            // overrides CN; defaults to dns_names[0]
  "subject": {                         // overrides leaf-subject defaults for this cert only
    "organization": "...",
    "organizational_unit": "...",
    "country": "...",
    "locality": "...",
    "province": "..."
  }
}
```

CN can be any string (no requirement that it appears in dns_names) — this is what enables CN=node/root for non-DNS principals.

Implementation: internal/api/api.go handleIssueCert reads new fields and passes them to internal/certengine/leaf.go IssueLeaf alongside dnsNames. Add CN/subject overrides to the LeafParams struct in internal/certengine/params.go.

## Acceptance
- POST /api/certificates with {"dns_names":["cockroachdb.example"],"common_name":"node"} produces a cert with Subject CN=node and SAN=cockroachdb.example
- Existing call shape (no common_name field) still produces CN=dns_names[0] — backwards compatible
- Subject override is per-cert and does not mutate the global leaf-subject defaults
- OpenAPI spec updated (depends on shushtls-5ism)

title.new: Allow per-certificate subject override (custom CN)
Accept CSR submission for certificate issuance

description.new:
## Why
The current POST /api/certificates flow has ShushTLS generate the private key server-side. This is convenient but:
- Forces callers to download the private key over HTTPS (extra exposure surface; key never stays local-only)
- Doesn't let callers control key algorithm/size (always ECDSA P-256)
- Doesn't let callers control subject fields beyond what the leaf-subject defaults expose
- Diverges from every other internal CA API (smallstep, Vault PKI, AWS PCA, cfssl) — agents trained on those have to relearn the shape

A CSR-based path lets callers generate the key locally, define their own subject and SANs in the CSR, and receive only the signed cert back. ShushTLS already has CSR plumbing — internal/certengine/csr.go exists and is used by the ACME finalize flow (internal/acme/server.go handleFinalize). Surface it on the REST API too.

## What
Add a new content-type branch to POST /api/certificates:
- `Content-Type: application/json` → existing flow (key generated server-side)
- `Content-Type: application/pkcs10` (or application/x-pem-file with a CSR PEM) → sign the submitted CSR

Validation: enforce that all SANs in the CSR are policy-allowed (whatever the existing policy is — review internal/certengine/csr.go for current behavior). Reject CSRs that include disallowed extensions (e.g. requesting CA:true).

Response: PEM-encoded leaf cert. No private key in response since the caller already has it.

## Acceptance
- POST /api/certificates with a PEM CSR body returns the signed certificate
- Returned cert's subject and SANs match the CSR
- The cert's profile (KU/EKU) still comes from LeafKeyUsages/LeafExtKeyUsages — caller cannot escalate
- GET /api/certificates/{primary_san}?type=tar still serves the bundle (use CN as primary_san if no SAN in CSR)

title: Accept CSR submission for certificate issuance

title.new: Bundle ergonomics: include CA, use generic filenames, optional k8s Secret output

description.new:
## Why
The current bundle from GET /api/certificates/{primary_san}?type=tar contains exactly two files named after the primary SAN:
- `{primary_san}.cert.pem`
- `{primary_san}.key.pem`

Two consequences for integrators:
1. Every script needs to know the primary SAN to find the files. Generic automation ("unpack and apply") needs an extra parameter or globbing.
2. The bundle does NOT include the root CA — every caller has to make a separate request to GET /api/ca/root.pem and stitch it together. Two HTTPS round-trips and two failure modes instead of one.

In the CockroachDB-on-k8s integration this manifested as a CronJob script that has to know the primary SAN per cert *and* fetch ca.crt separately *and* assemble three files into a Kubernetes Secret. With better bundle output it could be a four-line script.

## What
1. **Include the CA in the bundle.** Add `ca.pem` (root CA in PEM) to every tar/zip. Optional: also add `chain.pem` (cert+CA concatenated) for servers that prefer a chain file.

2. **Generic filenames.** Add a query parameter or new endpoint that uses canonical names: `cert.pem`, `key.pem`, `ca.pem` (or `chain.pem`). Keep the existing primary-SAN-named layout for backwards compat. Suggested API: `?names=generic` or a new bundle format.

3. **Optional Kubernetes Secret output.** `?format=k8s-tls` returns a ready-to-apply Secret YAML:
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
type: kubernetes.io/tls
metadata:
  name: {primary_san}
data:
  tls.crt: <base64>
  tls.key: <base64>
  ca.crt: <base64>
```
Lets a caller `curl ... | kubectl apply -f -` — no scripting needed.

Implementation: internal/api/api.go handleGetCert (around api.go:385) currently constructs the tar. Add the additional file(s) and the format switch there.

## Acceptance
- Default bundle (existing format) still works for backwards compat
- New default OR new format includes ca.pem alongside cert.pem and key.pem
- ?format=k8s-tls returns a valid Kubernetes Secret YAML that can be piped to `kubectl apply -f -`
- Documented in /docs

title: Bundle ergonomics: include CA, use generic filenames, optional k8s Secret output

description.new:
## Why
ShushTLS's ACME server intentionally accepts any challenge response — internal/acme/server.go marks authorizations valid without contacting the requesting client. This works for acme.sh and certbot (which post a challenge response and trust the server's verdict) but **fails for cert-manager** because cert-manager performs a *local* self-check before telling the ACME server to validate:

1. cert-manager creates a solver pod + Service + Ingress for the challenge
2. cert-manager itself issues GET http://{dnsname}/.well-known/acme-challenge/{token} from inside the cluster
3. Only if that succeeds does cert-manager POST to the ACME server's challenge URL

For internal DNS names (`*.svc.cluster.local`, `root`, etc.) the self-check fails because the names don't resolve and the cluster has no ingress controller covering them. ShushTLS would happily mark valid — but cert-manager never asks. The challenge stays Pending forever. A real integration session burned ~45 minutes here before falling back to the direct API.

## What — Documentation
Add a 'Using with cert-manager' page or section under /acme that:
- States plainly: cert-manager's HTTP-01 self-check must succeed before cert-manager talks to the ACME server. ShushTLS's permissive validation does not help.
- Lists the realistic workarounds in order of effort:
  a) Use cert-manager's DNS-01 with a webhook solver that is similarly permissive
  b) Use a CronJob hitting POST /api/certificates directly (sample manifest)
  c) Install an ingress controller and a CoreDNS wildcard rewrite so the self-check resolves to the solver
- Includes a working CronJob YAML example for option (b) — the working manifest from the CockroachDB session at ~/shushtls-cert-refresh.yaml can serve as a starting point

## What — Optional code path
Ship a tiny cert-manager DNS-01 webhook (separate repo: cert-manager-webhook-shushtls) that always presents/checks DNS records as 'present'. cert-manager skips its own DNS propagation check when using a webhook solver that returns Present without delay. This unlocks the ACME path end-to-end inside Kubernetes.

## Acceptance
- /acme page (template internal/ui/templates/acme.html) gains a 'Using with cert-manager' section covering the trap and at least one working workaround with a copy-pasteable manifest
- Webhook solver tracked as a separate follow-up issue if scope grows

## Why
The ACME directory URL is the entrypoint for every ACME client. It's served at `/acme/directory` (internal/acme/server.go:90 — `mux.HandleFunc("GET "+dir, s.handleDirectory)`) but the path is not stated on /docs.

The /acme page mentions the ACME server exists; /docs does not link to it; an agent has to either find /acme by following nav, or brute-force-probe common paths (`/acme/directory`, `/api/acme/directory`, `/acme/v1/directory`) to discover the right one. In testing it took four guesses.

Add or document gaps in ACME directory: revokeCert, keyChange

description.new:
## Why
The directory endpoint at internal/acme/server.go:107-111 returns only:
```
{
  "newAccount": "...",
  "newNonce": "...",
  "newOrder": "..."
}
```

RFC8555 §7.1.1 lists `newAccount`, `newNonce`, `newOrder`, `newAuthz` (optional), `revokeCert`, `keyChange` as standard directory fields. Stricter clients (e.g. Caddy, some Boulder forks) check for revokeCert/keyChange at boot and may refuse to start or warn loudly when absent.

## What
Either:
1. **Implement** the missing endpoints:
   - POST /acme/revokeCert — accept signed JWS, revoke the cert (mark revoked in store; ShushTLS doesn't need to do CRL/OCSP since this is a private CA, but the revocation should at least be recorded and the cert removed from the listed certs view)
   - POST /acme/keyChange — rotate account key (RFC8555 §7.3.5)
2. **Document** explicitly that they are not supported, with rationale (this is a home CA — revocation works by reissuing; account key rotation is out of scope).

Prefer option 1 even with stub-level implementations because RFC compliance reduces friction with strict clients.

## Acceptance
- Either the directory includes revokeCert and keyChange URLs that handle the standard request bodies, or /acme page lists which directory fields are intentionally omitted with rationale
- ACME server integration tests cover the chosen behavior

title: Add or document gaps in ACME directory: revokeCert, keyChange

description.new:
## Why
GET /api/certificates currently returns only:
```
[{
  "primary_san": "...",
  "dns_names": [...],
  "not_before": "...",
  "not_after": "...",
  "is_service": false
}]
```

To inspect anything else — Subject CN/O/OU, key algorithm, EKU, serial, SHA-256 fingerprint, signature algorithm — an agent has to download every bundle and run openssl. For inventory/audit use cases this is O(N) round-trips and bundle parsing instead of one JSON fetch.

## What
Extend the list response with cert metadata that's cheap to surface from the existing cert store (internal/certengine/store.go and the parsed cert in memory):
```
{
  "primary_san": "...",
  "common_name": "...",
  "dns_names": [...],
  "not_before": "...",
  "not_after": "...",
  "serial": "hex...",
  "sha256_fingerprint": "hex...",
  "key_algorithm": "ECDSA-P256",
  "signature_algorithm": "ECDSAWithSHA256",
  "key_usage": ["DigitalSignature"],
  "extended_key_usage": ["ServerAuth", "ClientAuth"],
  "is_service": false
}
```

These fields all come from x509.Certificate fields and are already parsed when the cert is loaded — no extra crypto work.

Optional: query param `?fields=basic` to keep the old shape, default to enriched.

## Acceptance
- GET /api/certificates returns the new fields for every cert
- Existing fields preserved (no breaking change for callers that look at primary_san / dns_names / not_after)
- OpenAPI spec reflects the richer shape (depends on shushtls-5ism)

description.new:
## Why
`shushtls.radical.org` (the deployment used in real-world testing) serves its API behind a TLS cert issued by its own Radical Artworks Root CA. Every external integration therefore needs the root CA pre-installed *before* it can talk to the API:

- curl needs `--cacert` or `-k` (and `-k` defeats the point)
- cert-manager's ACME ClusterIssuer needs `spec.acme.caBundle` populated with the root CA in base64
- Container images that call the API need the CA baked in or mounted
- Agents fetching /api/openapi.json (when it exists — shushtls-5ism) have to handle the trust dance first

The chicken-and-egg is real: 'install the CA' requires fetching it from the API, which requires trusting the CA.

## What — pick one
1. **Get a publicly-trusted cert for the API endpoint.** The host is on a public TLD (.radical.org). Use Let's Encrypt (acme.sh, certbot, or self-bootstrap via ShushTLS's own ACME pointed at LE) for the API endpoint only. Leaf certs ShushTLS issues stay on its own CA — only the front door changes. Eliminates the trust dance for every API consumer.

2. **Document the bootstrap dance explicitly.** A 'Bootstrapping trust' page that shows:
   - curl one-liner using --insecure to fetch /api/ca/root.pem
   - SHA-256 fingerprint of the root CA (printed alongside the cert in the UI) so callers can verify out-of-band
   - Examples for: docker container (COPY ca.pem and use SSL_CERT_FILE), Kubernetes (caBundle in ACME issuer, configMap for general use), curl (--cacert)

Option 1 is the better UX. Option 2 is the better minimum.

## Acceptance
- Either the API endpoint serves a publicly-trusted cert by default, or /docs has a 'Bootstrapping trust' section with copy-pasteable examples for the common environments above
- Root CA's SHA-256 fingerprint is displayed in the UI for out-of-band verification

description.new:
## Why
ShushTLS already ships one-line installers (`/api/ca/install/{macos,linux,windows}`) that wire the root CA into the OS trust store — and that is the right primary UX. But some callers can't or shouldn't lean on the OS trust store:

- **cert-manager** ACME ClusterIssuer needs the root CA inline as `spec.acme.caBundle` (base64). In a real integration this took a probe of `shushtls.radical.org` with openssl to discover the API endpoint is self-signed, then base64-encoding the root PEM.
- **Container images** built from minimal bases (distroless, scratch) have no trust store to install into — they need a CA file mounted or baked in.
- **Bootstrap scripts** before the OS installer has run (e.g. provisioning a fresh host) need a way to pin the CA out-of-band.

Today there's no docs section answering 'how do I trust ShushTLS from a place where I can't run the OS installer?' and no fingerprint published for out-of-band verification.

## What
Add to /docs (template: internal/ui/templates/docs.html) a short 'Trusting the CA from constrained environments' section covering:
- The canonical fetch: `curl --insecure https://host/api/ca/root.pem -o ca.pem` (the --insecure is the bootstrap; subsequent calls use --cacert ca.pem)
- The root CA SHA-256 fingerprint — also surface it in the web UI on the install/trust page so a human can compare out-of-band
- One worked example each for: cert-manager (caBundle base64), Docker (COPY + SSL_CERT_FILE), Kubernetes ConfigMap mount

Explicitly NOT in scope: serving the API behind a public CA. ShushTLS is the CA — eating its own dogfood is the design.

## Acceptance
- /docs has a 'Trusting the CA from constrained environments' section with the bootstrap fetch, fingerprint, and three worked examples
- Root CA SHA-256 fingerprint visible in the web UI alongside the install button

description.new:
## Why
The current POST /api/certificates flow generates the private key server-side and returns it in the bundle. That's the right default for the home-LAN use case where the CA operator and the device owner are the same person.

But two real scenarios on a home network can't use that flow:
1. **Hardware-backed keys** — a TPM-, Secure-Enclave-, or YubiKey-stored key can never leave the device. The device needs to generate the key locally and only ask ShushTLS to sign it.
2. **Workloads that generate their own keys** — Kubernetes pods using cert-manager with a private CSR, etcd nodes provisioning themselves, mTLS daemons that mint a fresh keypair on boot. They already have the key; they need a cert for it.

ShushTLS already has CSR plumbing internally — the ACME finalize path (internal/acme/server.go) uses internal/certengine/csr.go to sign caller-supplied CSRs. This issue exposes the same capability on the REST API.

## What
Add a content-type branch to POST /api/certificates:
- `Content-Type: application/json` → existing flow (key generated server-side). No change for existing callers.
- `Content-Type: application/pkcs10` or PEM body → sign the submitted CSR, return cert only (no private key in response).

Validation: enforce the existing leaf cert profile (LeafKeyUsages, LeafExtKeyUsages from internal/certengine/params.go) regardless of what the CSR requests. Reject CSRs with CA:true or unexpected extensions.

## Acceptance
- POST /api/certificates with a PEM-encoded CSR body returns the signed cert
- Returned cert's SANs match the CSR's SANs; profile (KU/EKU) comes from the engine constants, not the CSR
- Existing JSON flow unchanged

description.old:
## Why
The directory endpoint at internal/acme/server.go:107-111 returns only:
```
{
  "newAccount": "...",
  "newNonce": "...",
  "newOrder": "..."
}
```

RFC8555 §7.1.1 lists `newAccount`, `newNonce`, `newOrder`, `newAuthz` (optional), `revokeCert`, `keyChange` as standard directory fields. Stricter clients (e.g. Caddy, some Boulder forks) check for revokeCert/keyChange at boot and may refuse to start or warn loudly when absent.

## What
Either:
1. **Implement** the missing endpoints:
   - POST /acme/revokeCert — accept signed JWS, revoke the cert (mark revoked in store; ShushTLS doesn't need to do CRL/OCSP since this is a private CA, but the revocation should at least be recorded and the cert removed from the listed certs view)
   - POST /acme/keyChange — rotate account key (RFC8555 §7.3.5)
2. **Document** explicitly that they are not supported, with rationale (this is a home CA — revocation works by reissuing; account key rotation is out of scope).

Prefer option 1 even with stub-level implementations because RFC compliance reduces friction with strict clients.

## Acceptance
- Either the directory includes revokeCert and keyChange URLs that handle the standard request bodies, or /acme page lists which directory fields are intentionally omitted with rationale
- ACME server integration tests cover the chosen behavior
description.new:
## Why
The directory endpoint at internal/acme/server.go:107-111 returns only:
```
{
  "newAccount": "...",
  "newNonce": "...",
  "newOrder": "..."
}
```

RFC8555 §7.1.1 lists additional fields most public CAs publish: `revokeCert`, `keyChange`, `newAuthz` (optional). For a home CA the omissions are mostly fine — revocation in a controlled LAN is 'just reissue', account key rotation is low-value when the threat model is friendly.

But callers who check the directory (stricter ACME clients, anyone debugging an integration) currently have no way to tell whether the omissions are bugs or by design. This caused real confusion during a cert-manager integration session.

## What
Update /acme (template: internal/ui/templates/acme.html) with a short 'What's supported' table listing each RFC8555 directory field and its status: implemented, intentionally omitted (with rationale), or considered. Recommended copy for the omissions:

- **revokeCert**: not implemented. On a home network, revocation is achieved by reissuing the cert under the same SANs (the old cert is removed from /api/certificates list and supersedes via expiry/replacement). If you need revocation as a primary mechanism, you probably need a CA bigger than ShushTLS.
- **keyChange**: not implemented. Account key rotation is low-value when account-key compromise on a trusted LAN already implies broader trust compromise.
- **newAuthz**: not implemented. Pre-authorization is unnecessary because challenges are auto-validated.

If a stricter client refuses to start without these fields, file an issue.

## Acceptance
- /acme page renders a 'What's supported' table covering at minimum: newAccount, newNonce, newOrder, newAuthz, revokeCert, keyChange
- Each omission has a one-sentence rationale aligned with the home-LAN trust model
- No code change to the ACME server; this is a docs-only issue

description.new:
## Why

The HTTP API in `internal/api` is hand-written `net/http` handlers with ad-hoc JSON types and no machine-readable contract. The agent-accessibility epic needs OpenAPI 3.x at `/api/openapi.json` (shushtls-5ism). Migrating to [Huma v2](https://huma.rocks/) now — while the surface is ~15 routes — gives us spec generation, JSON Schema, optional `/docs`, and guard rails before the API grows.

## Scope

- `internal/api` only (not ACME — RFC 8555 has its own contract; link via OpenAPI `externalDocs` if needed)
- Keep existing `certengine` / `auth` integration; swap routing layer to Huma (`humago` on stdlib `ServeMux`)
- Satisfy shushtls-5ism for REST endpoints via generated spec (not hand-maintained YAML)

## Out of scope (follow-ups)

- ACME endpoints in OpenAPI
- New API features (CSR, enriched list, etc.) — migrate parity first

acceptance.new:
- All current `/api/*` REST endpoints behave as before (status codes, auth rules, binary responses)
- `GET /api/openapi.json` returns valid OpenAPI 3.x covering every migrated endpoint
- `go test ./internal/api/...` passes (updated for Huma)
- `/docs` links to OpenAPI (or Huma docs UI mounted under `/api/docs` — decide in implementation)
